sources.notes: Read the full statement and constraints for coloring all edges of K_(2n,m) with n colors and forbidding monochromatic cycles.
Official editorial confirms the tight m <= 2n-1 forest bound and the cyclic pair-coloring construction.
Actually read; independently derives the forest edge bound and cross-checks the shifted cyclic construction.
Re-derived each color class as a subdivided path and exhaustively found no monochromatic cycle in all 900 feasible pairs with 1 <= n <= 30.

annotation.summary: Decide when every edge of K_(2n,m) can be colored with n colors so that every monochromatic subgraph is acyclic, and construct such a coloring.
annotation.constraints: Up to 100 test cases; 1 <= n,m <= 1000; both the sum of n and the sum of m are at most 1000; output contains 2nm colors for each feasible case.
annotation.core_idea: Every color class must be a forest on 2n+m vertices, so it has at most 2n+m-1 edges. Across n colors this forces 2nm <= n(2n+m-1), hence m <= 2n-1. This is also sufficient. Using zero-based left index i and right index j, output color(i,j)=floor(((i+j) mod 2n)/2)+1. Fix a color c. At right vertex j, exactly the two left residues 2(c-1)-j and 2(c-1)+1-j modulo 2n receive c. Suppress that degree-two right vertex into an edge between those two consecutive residues. As j runs from 0 through 2n-2, these projected edges are all but one edge of the 2n-cycle, hence a path; the original monochromatic graph is its edge subdivision and is therefore a tree. For smaller m, taking the first m columns leaves a subforest. Thus the construction works exactly when m <= 2n-1.
annotation.complexity: O(nm) time, which is optimal up to constants because 2nm colors must be printed, and O(1) auxiliary memory apart from buffered output.
tricks: Bound every color class by the maximum number of edges in a forest.
Construct the extremal case m=2n-1 and truncate columns for smaller m.
Suppress each right vertex into an edge between its two same-color left neighbors.
Use shifted consecutive residue pairs so every color projects to a path.

solution_variants.name: forest-bound-cyclic-path-construction
solution_variants.summary: Reject m>2n-1 by summing forest edge bounds. Otherwise color edge (i,j) by floor(((i+j) mod 2n)/2)+1; for each fixed color the right vertices subdivide consecutive edges of a path on the left vertices.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nm) time and O(1) auxiliary memory apart from output.

tags.evidence: The condition is exactly a partition of all edges into n acyclic color classes; both the impossibility bound and the extremal construction reason about those forests.
tags.description: Partition graph edges into forests, minimize or bound the number of forests needed, or exploit edge-count constraints of such a forest decomposition; exclude ordinary proper edge coloring where each color class is a matching.
tags.created_reason: CF2048E is centrally an edge partition into forests. The existing graph/edge-coloring tag describes proper edge coloring into matchings, which is a different condition and would misclassify this problem.

tags.evidence: After proving the feasibility threshold, the solution must explicitly output a cyclic color formula for all 2nm edges and prove every color class acyclic.
The necessary and exact feasibility threshold follows from 2nm <= n(2n+m-1), which simplifies to m <= 2n-1.
The object being decomposed is K_(2n,m), and each right vertex becomes the subdivision vertex of one projected left-side edge for every color.
At m=2n-1 every monochromatic class is proved to be a spanning subdivided path, hence a tree; smaller m gives a subforest.
The construction shifts consecutive residue pairs modulo 2n so the projected edge for each color advances once around a cycle.
